questions.question: How does HashMap work internally in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: HashMap stores entries in an array of buckets. The bucket index is computed as hash(key) & (capacity-1). Collisions are resolved via chaining (linked list), which Java 8 converts to a red-black tree when a bucket exceeds 8 entries. The map resizes (doubles) when the load factor (default 0.75) is exceeded.
keyPoints: hash() applies a secondary hash to spread bits — reduces clustering from poor hashCode() implementations
Treeification at 8 entries per bucket changes worst-case from O(n) to O(log n)
Resize doubles capacity and rehashes all entries — O(n) operation amortized to O(1)
Not thread-safe — use ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent access
null key is always stored in bucket 0; null values are allowed

questions.question: What is the contract between equals() and hashCode()?
questions.quickAnswer: If two objects are equal via equals(), they must return the same hashCode(). The reverse is not required — two unequal objects can share a hash code (collision). Violating this breaks HashMap, HashSet, and any hash-based collection.
keyPoints: Equal objects → same hashCode (mandatory). Same hashCode → not necessarily equal (allowed collision)
If you override equals() without overriding hashCode(), HashMap.get() may return null even after put()
IDE-generated equals/hashCode is usually correct; use Objects.equals() and Objects.hash() to handle nulls
Mutable fields in hashCode create bugs when the object is stored in a HashSet then mutated
Lombok @EqualsAndHashCode and Java records auto-generate correct implementations

questions.question: What is the String pool and how does string interning work?
questions.quickAnswer: String literals are stored in the String pool (part of heap in Java 8+). Two literals with the same value share the same object, so == works. Strings created with new String() bypass the pool. String.intern() explicitly adds a string to the pool and returns the pooled reference.
keyPoints: String pool lives in the heap (metaspace pre-Java 8) — prevents duplicate literal objects
String literals are automatically interned; new String("x") creates a separate heap object
s1 == s2 works for literals but NEVER use == for string comparison in application code
intern() is useful when storing millions of repeated strings (reduces memory) but has overhead
String is immutable — all string operations return new objects; pool only covers literals and interned strings
questions.codeExample:
String a = "hello";         // pool
String b = "hello";         // same pool object
String c = new String("hello"); // separate heap object
System.out.println(a == b); // true
System.out.println(a == c); // false
System.out.println(a == c.intern()); // true

questions.question: When should you use StringBuilder vs StringBuffer?
questions.quickAnswer: Use StringBuilder in single-threaded code — it has no synchronization overhead. StringBuffer is thread-safe (synchronized methods) but slower. In practice, StringBuffer is rarely needed because string building is usually done in a single thread or protected externally.
keyPoints: StringBuilder: not thread-safe, faster — use in 99% of cases
StringBuffer: thread-safe (every method synchronized), use only when shared across threads without external sync
Both use a resizable char array internally; initial capacity 16, doubles when exceeded
Java compiler automatically converts string concatenation in loops to StringBuilder
StringJoiner and String.join() are cleaner for delimiter-separated lists

questions.question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
questions.quickAnswer: Checked exceptions (extend Exception, not RuntimeException) must be declared with throws or caught — the compiler enforces this. Unchecked exceptions (extend RuntimeException) can be thrown anywhere without declaration. Checked exceptions represent recoverable external failures; unchecked represent programming errors.
keyPoints: Checked: IOException, SQLException — caller must handle or declare; enforces error handling at compile time
Unchecked: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException — signals programmer errors, not worth forcing callers to handle
Modern Java practice (and most frameworks) prefer unchecked — checked exceptions leak implementation details and pollute APIs
Spring converts checked SQLExceptions to unchecked DataAccessException hierarchy
Always catch the most specific exception type first in multi-catch blocks

questions.question: How does try-with-resources work?
questions.quickAnswer: try-with-resources automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable at the end of the block, even if an exception is thrown. Multiple resources are closed in reverse order of declaration. If both the try body and close() throw exceptions, the close() exception is suppressed.
keyPoints: Resource must implement AutoCloseable (or Closeable for IO); close() is called automatically
Closes in reverse order: last opened, first closed — mirrors stack semantics
Suppressed exceptions: if try throws and close() also throws, the close() exception is attached via addSuppressed()
Replaces verbose finally blocks that could swallow the original exception
Java 9+: can use existing effectively-final variables in the resource list

questions.question: What are the different uses of the final keyword?
questions.quickAnswer: final on a variable means it can only be assigned once (blank final must be assigned in constructor). final on a method prevents overriding. final on a class prevents subclassing (e.g., String). Lambda parameters captured from outer scope must be effectively final.
keyPoints: final field: must be assigned at declaration or in every constructor path; prevents reassignment (not deep immutability)
final method: cannot be overridden in subclasses; useful for template method pattern invariants
final class: cannot be extended — String, Integer, and all wrapper types are final
Effectively final (Java 8+): variable never reassigned even without final keyword — can be used in lambdas
final does NOT make object immutable — it prevents reference reassignment but the object itself can still be mutated

questions.question: How does the static keyword work in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: static members belong to the class, not instances — they're shared across all objects. Static methods can't access instance members (no this). Static initializer blocks run once when the class is loaded, in order of appearance. Static nested classes don't hold a reference to the outer class.
keyPoints: Static fields: one copy per class, not per instance — shared state (use carefully in multithreaded code)
Static methods: no access to instance fields or this; good for utility/factory methods
Static initializer blocks: run at class loading, in top-to-bottom order, before any constructor
Static nested class vs inner class: static nested has no implicit outer reference (no memory leak risk)
Static import: import static java.util.Collections.sort; allows calling sort() without class prefix

questions.question: What is the var keyword and where can't it be used?
questions.quickAnswer: var (Java 10+) enables local variable type inference — the compiler infers the type from the right-hand side. It's resolved at compile time, not runtime, so there's no performance difference. It cannot be used for fields, method parameters, return types, or when the right side is null or a lambda.
keyPoints: Compile-time type inference only — bytecode is identical to using the explicit type
Cannot use with: fields, method params, return types, catch parameters (Java <10), null literals, array initializers without new
Good for: long generic types (Map.Entry<String, List<Integer>>), local variables with obvious types
Avoid for: non-obvious types where the type name aids readability
var is not a reserved word — it's a context-sensitive identifier, so var var = 5 compiles
questions.codeExample:
var list = new ArrayList<String>(); // List<String> inferred
var entry = map.entrySet().iterator().next(); // cleaner

// Cannot use:
var x = null;        // error: can't infer type
var fn = s -> s.length(); // error: lambda needs target type
private var field;   // error: not for fields

questions.question: What are the pitfalls of autoboxing and unboxing?
questions.quickAnswer: Autoboxing converts primitives to wrapper objects (int → Integer) silently. The main pitfalls are: NullPointerException when unboxing a null wrapper, unexpected == comparison between Integer objects outside the cache range (-128 to 127), and performance overhead from repeated boxing in loops.
keyPoints: NullPointerException: Integer i = null; int x = i; // NPE at unbox
== compares references for Integer objects outside cache range: Integer.valueOf(200) == Integer.valueOf(200) is false
Performance: autoboxing in tight loops creates many short-lived objects — use primitive streams (IntStream) instead
Method overloading ambiguity: remove(int index) vs remove(Object o) on List — autoboxing can call the wrong one
Use Integer.equals() or Objects.equals() for null-safe comparison; avoid == on wrapper types

questions.question: Why does Integer cache work for values -128 to 127?
questions.quickAnswer: Integer.valueOf() caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127 (JVM spec minimum). When autoboxing or calling valueOf() in this range, the same cached object is returned — so == works. Outside this range, new objects are created each time.
keyPoints: Cache is initialized at class loading — 256 Integer objects pre-created
Upper bound is configurable via JVM flag: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=<size>; lower bound always -128
Only Integer is guaranteed to cache by the JVM spec; other wrappers (Long, Short, Byte, Character) may also cache
Boolean caches only TRUE and FALSE; Character caches 0-127
This is why you should never use == to compare Integer values — always use .equals()
questions.codeExample:
Integer a = Integer.valueOf(100); // cached
Integer b = Integer.valueOf(100); // same object
System.out.println(a == b); // true

Integer x = Integer.valueOf(200); // new object
Integer y = Integer.valueOf(200); // another new object  
System.out.println(x == y); // false
System.out.println(x.equals(y)); // true — always use equals

questions.question: Is Java pass-by-value or pass-by-reference?
questions.quickAnswer: Java is always pass-by-value. For primitives, the value is copied. For objects, the reference (memory address) is copied — so you can mutate the object through the parameter, but reassigning the parameter doesn't affect the caller's variable.
keyPoints: Primitives: copy of value — no way to modify the original through the parameter
Objects: copy of the reference — both caller and method point to the same object, mutations are visible
Reassigning the parameter (param = new Object()) only changes the local copy — caller is unaffected
This means: you can change object state (list.add()), but can't change which object the caller points to
C++ has true pass-by-reference (& parameters); Java has no equivalent

questions.question: What are the different memory areas in the JVM?
questions.quickAnswer: The JVM has: Heap (objects and arrays, GC-managed), Stack (one per thread, method frames with local variables), Metaspace (class metadata, static variables in Java 8+, replaces PermGen), PC Register (current instruction pointer per thread), and Native Method Stack (for JNI calls).
keyPoints: Heap: all objects and arrays live here; divided into Young Gen (Eden + Survivor) and Old Gen
Stack: one per thread; each frame holds local vars, operand stack, method reference; StackOverflowError on too-deep recursion
Metaspace: replaces PermGen in Java 8; grows dynamically by default; stores class metadata, static fields
String pool moved to heap in Java 7 (was PermGen) — enables GC of unreferenced interned strings
OutOfMemoryError: heap exhausted; StackOverflowError: stack exhausted (infinite recursion)

questions.question: How does garbage collection work in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: The JVM uses generational GC based on the weak generational hypothesis: most objects die young. New objects go to Eden (Young Gen). Surviving minor GCs are promoted to Survivor spaces, then Old Gen. The GC traces live objects from GC roots (stack refs, static fields), marking reachable objects and collecting the rest.
keyPoints: GC roots: local variables on stacks, static fields, JNI references — these anchor the reachability graph
Minor GC: collects Young Gen (Eden + Survivors) — fast, frequent, stop-the-world pause is short
Major/Full GC: collects Old Gen — slow, causes longer stop-the-world pauses
G1GC (default Java 9+): divides heap into regions, collects high-garbage regions first (Garbage First)
ZGC/Shenandoah: concurrent collectors with sub-millisecond pauses for large heaps

questions.question: What is the difference between WeakReference, SoftReference, and PhantomReference?
questions.quickAnswer: WeakReference: object is GC-eligible as soon as no strong references exist — collected on next GC. SoftReference: object is kept until JVM needs memory — good for caches. PhantomReference: object is already collected when the reference is enqueued — used for cleanup/finalization.
keyPoints: Strong reference (normal): obj = new Object() — never collected while referenced
WeakReference: WeakHashMap uses this — entries collected when key has no strong references
SoftReference: JVM clears soft refs before throwing OutOfMemoryError — ideal for memory-sensitive caches
PhantomReference: get() always returns null; used with ReferenceQueue for post-GC cleanup (replacing finalize())
All three can be used with ReferenceQueue to receive notification when the referent is about to be or has been collected

questions.question: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
questions.quickAnswer: Comparable defines the natural ordering of a class (implemented by the class itself, single sort order). Comparator defines an external ordering (separate class/lambda, multiple sort orders). Collections.sort() and TreeSet use natural ordering; overloads accept a Comparator for custom ordering.
keyPoints: Comparable: class implements it (compareTo), defines one natural order — e.g., String, Integer, Date
Comparator: external strategy object, multiple can exist for the same type
Comparator.comparing(keyExtractor) with thenComparing() chains multiple sort criteria cleanly
If compareTo/compare returns 0, equals() should ideally return true (required by SortedSet/SortedMap)
Comparator.naturalOrder() / reverseOrder() are convenience instances

questions.question: What is the difference between Iterator and ListIterator?
questions.quickAnswer: Iterator traverses any Collection in one direction (forward only) and supports remove(). ListIterator works only on Lists, supports bidirectional traversal (hasPrevious/previous), and allows add/set operations during iteration in addition to remove().
keyPoints: Iterator: hasNext(), next(), remove() — works on any Iterable
ListIterator: adds hasPrevious(), previous(), add(), set(), nextIndex(), previousIndex()
Both throw ConcurrentModificationException if the list is structurally modified outside the iterator
ListIterator.add() inserts before the cursor position; set() replaces the last element returned
For-each loop uses Iterator under the hood — ConcurrentModificationException applies there too

questions.question: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
questions.quickAnswer: Fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified after the iterator is created. Fail-safe iterators (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap) operate on a snapshot or allow concurrent modification — no exception, but may not see latest changes.
keyPoints: Fail-fast: modCount check — if modCount changes after iterator creation, CME is thrown on next()
Not guaranteed: CME is thrown on best-effort basis — don't rely on it for thread-safety
CopyOnWriteArrayList: iterator operates on snapshot taken at creation; writes create new array
ConcurrentHashMap iterator: weakly consistent — reflects some but not necessarily all updates
To safely remove during iteration: use iterator.remove() (doesn't trigger CME)

questions.question: What is the difference between Collections.unmodifiableList, List.of, and Collections.emptyList?
questions.quickAnswer: Collections.unmodifiableList wraps an existing list — the original can still be mutated and changes are visible through the wrapper. List.of() creates a truly immutable list (no mutation via any reference). Collections.emptyList() returns a singleton empty immutable list.
keyPoints: unmodifiableList: view-only wrapper, not a copy — mutations to the original list are visible; throws UnsupportedOperationException on write attempts via the wrapper
List.of() (Java 9+): immutable, no nulls allowed, not a view — truly unchangeable
Collections.emptyList(): singleton, returns same object every time, very memory efficient
List.copyOf() (Java 10+): immutable copy — safe from original mutations
All three throw UnsupportedOperationException on add/remove/set attempts

questions.question: What are Java records and when should you use them?
questions.quickAnswer: Records (Java 16+) are immutable data carriers. Declaring record Point(int x, int y) auto-generates: constructor, getters (x(), y()), equals(), hashCode(), and toString(). Use them for DTOs, value objects, and data transfer — not for entities needing mutable state or inheritance.
keyPoints: Auto-generated: canonical constructor, component accessors (not getX() — just x()), equals/hashCode by all components, toString
All fields are final — records are shallowly immutable (a List field's contents can still be mutated)
Cannot extend other classes (implicitly extends Record); can implement interfaces
Can add custom constructors (compact constructor for validation), static fields/methods, instance methods
Ideal for: DTOs, API request/response bodies, Map keys, pattern matching in switch
